title: AI Model Co-Design: Hardware-Friendly LLM Design | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA architects lay out concrete rules (near-square, tile-aligned linear layers; width-over-depth; NVFP4 quantization; wide expert parallelism; Helix/chunked pipeline parallelism) for designing transformer LLMs that map efficiently onto Blackwell GPU hardware, arguing that model-hardware co-design — not just bigger chips — is what determines whether frontier LLMs actually reach the throughput/latency Pareto frontier, effectively asking the industry's model builders to design around NVIDIA's tile geometry and precision formats.

insights.claim: AI system performance reduces to three dimensions that must be balanced together — accuracy, throughput (tokens/sec for the datacenter), and interactivity (user-perceived responsiveness, dominated by latency) — because high accuracy is wasted if responses are slow and raw throughput means little if individual user experience is laggy.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Establishes the mental model NVIDIA uses to frame every subsequent LLM-serving optimization decision; useful for investors assessing whether a given inference-optimization claim (e.g., a competitor's benchmark) is cherry-picking one dimension while sacrificing another.
insights.quote: Deployments must balance all three: High accuracy is wasted if responses are slow, and raw throughput means little if each user’s experience is laggy.

insights.claim: Holding accuracy fixed, throughput and interactivity form a two-dimensional Pareto frontier where improving one typically costs the other; the design goal is to push the entire frontier outward rather than move along a fixed curve.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: This Pareto framing is the analytical lens for evaluating whether a new NVIDIA software/hardware release (e.g., a new TensorRT-LLM feature) represents genuine frontier expansion versus a mere tradeoff shift.
insights.locator: Figure 1, throughput vs interactivity Pareto frontier

insights.claim: Amdahl's law dictates where optimization effort pays off: if attention consumes 77% of runtime in a given workload regime, optimizing feed-forward layers yields only marginal gains, so engineers must identify their dominant bottleneck before optimizing.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: A generalizable prioritization principle for any team optimizing LLM serving costs — avoid over-investing engineering effort into non-bottleneck components.
insights.quote: Amdahl’s law applies: optimizing one part helps only as much as the time it occupies. If attention is 77% of runtime, tuning the feed-forward layers yields only marginal gains; the attention path is where effort pays off.

insights.claim: The author states plainly that models aligned with the underlying hardware 'don't just run faster; they scale better, cost less, and reach wider adoption,' framing hardware-aware model design as a commercial adoption lever, not merely a performance nicety.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Directly ties engineering choices to business outcomes (cost, adoption) — a thesis statement that frames NVIDIA's entire co-design series as aimed at influencing how model builders design LLMs to favor NVIDIA hardware characteristics.
insights.quote: The idea is simple: models that align with the hardware don’t just run faster; they scale better, cost less, and reach wider adoption.

insights.claim: Achievable GPU performance is bounded by the roofline model: workloads with low arithmetic intensity (compute ops per byte moved) are memory-bandwidth-bound, while high-arithmetic-intensity workloads are compute-bound; maximizing throughput requires pushing workloads into the compute-bound region, while latency-sensitive decoding runs at low concurrency and stays memory-bound.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: The roofline model is the foundational lens the rest of the article's guidelines derive from — essential background for interpreting any GPU throughput/latency claim.

insights.claim: In a deliberately small-H' FFN-2 layer (H'=512, H=8192) on GB300 with FP4 inputs/FP8 outputs, memory-transfer time exceeds compute time at every tested token count (256 to 16,384), keeping the layer memory-bound throughout regardless of batch size, because the small reduction dimension limits arithmetic intensity.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Empirically proves that increasing batch size alone cannot fix a poorly-shaped model layer — model architecture choices are as important as serving-time batching for GPU utilization.
insights.quote: Memory time exceeds math time at every token count, so the layer stays memory-bound throughout.

insights.claim: On GB300 silicon, NVFP4 GEMM throughput saturates near a reduction dimension (K) of ~6144, requiring K > 3072 just to reach 80% of sustained throughput; similarly, throughput saturates near a projection dimension (N) of ~6144, requiring N > 2560 to reach 80% of sustained throughput.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Gives model architects concrete numeric thresholds (K>3072, N>2560) to hit for near-peak Blackwell utilization — directly actionable design guidance that ties LLM architecture decisions to hardware efficiency.

insights.claim: Guideline: for a fixed parameter budget, favor near-square weight matrices and avoid making either the projection or reduction dimension too small, since arithmetic intensity for a square GEMM grows with H, and small H or H' wastes GPU time on data movement rather than math.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: A directly actionable model-architecture rule of thumb for LLM builders wanting maximum throughput per GPU-dollar on NVIDIA hardware.
insights.quote: Guideline 1: For a fixed-parameter model, favor near-square weight matrices and avoid making either the projection or reduction dimension too small.

insights.claim: GPUs execute GEMMs by splitting output matrices into tiles computed per streaming multiprocessor (SM), and recent GPUs let SMs cooperate on larger tiles via clusterMMA (two SMs) and Cooperative Grid Arrays (CGA, a full SM cluster); when a model dimension isn't a multiple of the resulting effective tile size, edge tiles run at full compute cost while only partially filled, wasting cycles ('tile quantization').
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Explains a non-obvious hardware-software interaction (tile quantization waste) that can silently cost throughput even when arithmetic intensity looks favorable — a subtle but material efficiency factor for anyone training/serving custom model architectures on Blackwell.
insights.quote: When a dimension is not a multiple of that effective tile, the edge tiles are only partially filled yet still launch and run a full tile’s worth of compute; the unused (padded) portion does no useful work, wasting cycles and lowering throughput.

insights.claim: Guideline: model dimensions should be multiples of 128 at minimum (a safe portable floor) and preferably 256 or 512 to align with the larger cooperative tiles formed by clusterMMA and CGA and capture maximum throughput.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: A precise, checkable design rule (multiples of 128/256/512) that model architects can directly encode into their model configs to avoid leaving Blackwell throughput on the table.
insights.quote: Guideline 2: Make model dimensions multiples of 128 at minimum to align with GPU tile sizes and cache-line widths, and prefer 256 or 512 to match the larger tiles formed by clusterMMA and CGA.

insights.claim: For a fixed parameter budget, wider transformer models offer higher arithmetic intensity and lower latency than deeper models through greater weight reuse and a shorter sequential critical path, making width advantageous for both throughput- and latency-oriented serving goals — though depth still contributes representational power, so there is a useful width-to-depth band rather than 'wider is always better.'
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: A contrarian-leaning design signal for LLM architecture researchers: pure depth scaling (common historically) is less hardware-efficient than width scaling under a fixed parameter budget, which could influence next-generation model architecture choices industry-wide if adopted broadly by model labs targeting NVIDIA infrastructure.
insights.quote: Guideline 3: When given a choice, prefer fewer, larger operations over many smaller ones; this maximizes arithmetic intensity and improves hardware utilization, benefiting both throughput and interactivity. In other words, wider transformer models are more hardware-friendly than deeper ones.
insights.locator: Wider models are more hardware-friendly than deeper models

themes.theme: NVFP4 quantization as a performance lever

insights.claim: NVFP4 applies a fine-grained FP8 (E4M3) scale to each 16-value micro-block plus a second-level FP32 scale per tensor, a hierarchical scaling scheme that sharply reduces quantization error while retaining the speed of 4-bit computation.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Explains the technical mechanism behind NVFP4's accuracy/speed tradeoff, relevant for assessing whether NVIDIA's proprietary low-precision format offers a durable edge over generic FP4/INT4 approaches used by competitors.
insights.quote: NVFP4 is designed specifically for balancing model accuracy and speed; it applies a fine-grained FP8 (E4M3) scale to each 16-value micro-block, plus a second-level FP32 scale per tensor.

insights.claim: DeepSeek-R1 accuracy under NVFP4 quantization stays within about one point of the FP8 baseline on most benchmarks, and matches or exceeds FP8 on SciCode, Math-500, and AIME 2024.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Concrete accuracy-parity evidence that NVFP4 is production-viable for frontier reasoning models, supporting NVIDIA's push for the format's adoption as a de facto low-precision inference standard.

insights.claim: NVIDIA provides end-to-end tooling — TensorRT Model Optimizer and LLM Compressor — supporting post-training quantization, quantization-aware training, and advanced calibration to quantize models to NVFP4 with minimal accuracy loss.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Reinforces NVIDIA's full-stack strategy: proprietary numeric format (NVFP4) plus proprietary tooling to make adoption low-friction, deepening ecosystem lock-in versus open, hardware-agnostic quantization tooling.

insights.claim: Guideline: when introducing computationally expensive operations into a network, model designers should consider whether those operations can be quantized at deployment, since designing layers that benefit from low-precision execution is key to extracting maximum throughput from modern GPUs.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Encourages model architects to design for quantizability from the start rather than retrofitting it, effectively asking the industry to co-design around NVIDIA's precision formats early in model development.
insights.quote: Guideline 4: When introducing computationally expensive operations into a network, consider whether they can be quantized at deployment.

themes.theme: Expert and pipeline parallelism for MoE scale-out

insights.claim: For throughput-oriented MoE serving, expert parallelism (running attention data-parallel and distributing FFN experts across GPUs) is favored over tensor parallelism because tensor parallelism requires an expensive AllReduce whose overhead grows with concurrency and drags down throughput, while data-parallel attention scales concurrency more naturally as GPUs are added.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: A concrete architectural recommendation (EP over TP for throughput-oriented MoE) that shapes how large-scale MoE inference deployments should be configured on NVIDIA infrastructure, with direct cost/throughput implications for cloud AI providers.
insights.quote: Using data parallelism (DP) for attention avoids a key limitation of tensor parallelism (TP), the expensive AllReduce needed to combine partial results.

insights.claim: Because per-GPU concurrency is capped by KV cache footprint, widening expert parallelism across more GPUs is the main lever for raising GEMM-M (and thus GPU utilization) in sparse MoE models; wider EP also increases aggregate memory bandwidth for loading expert weights and reduces per-GPU memory footprint, freeing memory for higher effective concurrency.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Explains the mechanism by which scaling GPU count directly improves MoE inference efficiency (not just capacity), a rationale investors can use to understand why large MoE model deployments favor wide multi-node GPU clusters over fewer, larger single nodes.

insights.claim: NVIDIA's Wide-EP feature in TensorRT-LLM addresses the two core challenges of expert parallelism — all-to-all communication overhead and expert load imbalance — via high-performance all-to-all kernels that scale EP across Blackwell multi-node NVLink systems and an adaptive load balancer that redistributes expert traffic in real time under skewed token/expert distributions.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: A specific, named NVIDIA software feature (Wide-EP) directly tied to Blackwell NVLink multi-node scaling — a competitive moat claim since it requires NVIDIA's specific interconnect topology to function at scale.

insights.claim: Chunked Pipeline Parallelism (CPP) splits both model layers across GPUs and input context into chunks flowing through the pipeline, letting prefill workers handle long sequences within tight first-token-latency budgets without resorting to wide tensor parallelism; in a DeepSeek-R1 example at 256K input length, increasing pipeline-parallel size reduces first-token latency while tokens/s/GPU stays roughly constant.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a concrete technique (CPP) for cutting latency on very long-context workloads (256K tokens) without sacrificing throughput — directly relevant to the economics of serving long-context frontier models at scale.

insights.claim: Guideline: models should be designed with regular, repeatable layer patterns that partition evenly, since Chunked Pipeline Parallelism only pays off when pipeline stages are balanced — imbalanced stages introduce idle-GPU pipeline bubbles.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Another concrete architecture-level design constraint (regular layer patterns) that model builders should adopt if optimizing for NVIDIA's pipeline-parallel serving infrastructure.
insights.quote: Guideline 6: Design models with regular, repeatable layer patterns that are easy to split into balanced pipeline stages.

insights.claim: Helix Parallelism (NVIDIA Research, 2025) solves the KV-cache tensor-parallelism ceiling — which is capped at the number of KV heads, beyond which the cache must be redundantly replicated — by sharding the KV cache across the sequence dimension during attention and reusing the same GPUs for TPxEP in the FFN layers; it is supported in TensorRT-LLM.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: A named proprietary NVIDIA parallelism technique (Helix) that directly addresses a scaling bottleneck (few KV heads in modern MQA/GQA/MLA models) increasingly relevant as models adopt fewer KV heads for efficiency — another software moat tied to NVIDIA's stack.
insights.locator: Hybrid parallel strategies to meet latency-oriented service goals

insights.claim: Hybrid parallelism schemes like Helix introduce additional communication overhead, but Blackwell NVL72's high-bandwidth NVLink fabric absorbs much of that cost, with the remainder overlappable with computation.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Directly credits NVLink fabric bandwidth as the enabling factor for advanced hybrid parallelism strategies, reinforcing that NVIDIA's interconnect (not just GPU compute) is central to its LLM-serving competitive advantage.
insights.locator: Hybrid parallel strategies to meet latency-oriented service goals

insights.claim: The article closes with a four-point design checklist for next-generation LLMs: keep dimensions near-square and aligned to 128 (ideally 256); favor width over depth; design for NVFP4 low-precision execution; and use regular, repeatable layer patterns for pipeline parallelism — framed as the first post in a series with subsequent posts covering compute bottlenecks, frictionless datacenter deployment, use-case matching, and scaling in practice.
insights.speaker: Ritika Borkar
insights.so_what: Signals NVIDIA is running a multi-part content/influence campaign aimed at shaping how the entire industry designs next-generation LLM architectures around NVIDIA's specific hardware characteristics — worth tracking subsequent posts in the series as a proxy for NVIDIA's evolving inference-stack strategy.
insights.quote: Small choices like these can meaningfully raise GPU utilization, delivering faster inference, higher throughput, and better interactivity from the same hardware.
